intro:
The Books of Kings (1–2 Kings, originally a single book) are anonymous. They narrate Israel and Judah from the death of David to the Babylonian exile, evaluating each king by faithfulness to the LORD.

Jewish tradition (the Talmud, Baba Bathra 15a) ascribed the work to the prophet Jeremiah, whose lifetime overlapped its closing events. Most readers since have seen it instead as the work of an anonymous prophetic compiler — the so-called ‘Deuteronomistic’ historian — who gathered older court and prophetic records into a single, theologically shaped history.

sections.title: The sources it names
sections.body: The author repeatedly cites earlier documents he drew upon: the Book of the Acts of Solomon, the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel, and the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah. The finished book is therefore openly a compilation from royal annals and prophetic memoirs, not an eyewitness diary.

sections.title: Date and purpose
sections.body: Kings ends with the release of the exiled king Jehoiachin (c. 561 BC), so its final form comes from the exile. Writing to a defeated, displaced people, the author explains the catastrophe: the exile was the covenant consequence of generations of idolatry, while God's promise to David still flickers in Jehoiachin's preservation.
